25.reason: [The condition `(c=(fgetc(in)))` treats EOF (which is -1) as true, causing infinite loop or undefined behavior. Should check `c != EOF` explicitly. Also, fgetc returns int, not char, so `c` should be int to correctly detect EOF.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-1: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [The condition `(c=(fgetc(in)))` treats EOF (which is -1) as true, causing infinite loop or undefined behavior. Should check `c != EOF` explicitly. Also, fgetc returns int, not char, so `c` should be int to correctly detect EOF.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-3: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [The condition `(c=(fgetc(in)))` treats EOF (which is -1) as true, causing infinite loop or undefined behavior. Also, fgetc returns int, not char, so storing in char loses EOF distinction.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [The loop condition treats EOF (typically -1) as true, causing infinite loop or undefined behavior. fgetc returns int, not char, so c should be int to correctly detect EOF.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [The condition (c = fgetc(in)) treats EOF (typically -1) as true, causing an infinite loop. Also, fgetc returns int, not char, so storing in char loses distinction between EOF and valid byte values.]

37.reason: [Same issue: `c` is declared as char, but fgetc returns int. Assigning EOF to a char may cause it to become positive (e.g., 255), breaking the loop condition.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-1: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [Same issue: `c` is declared as char, but fgetc returns int. Assigning EOF to a char may cause it to become positive (e.g., 255), breaking the loop condition.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-3: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [Same issue: c is declared as char, but fgetc returns int. When EOF is returned, it gets truncated when stored in char, leading to incorrect comparison and potential infinite loop.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [Same issue: c is declared as char, but fgetc returns int. When char is unsigned, EOF (-1) becomes 255 and never equals EOF, causing incorrect loop termination.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [Same issue: c is declared as char, but fgetc returns int. When EOF is read, it gets truncated to unsigned char range, making the !=EOF check unreliable.]

60.reason: [This assignment `r = temp` only changes the local pointer `r`, not the global `head`. Should assign `head = temp` when inserting at front.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-1: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [This assignment `r = temp` only changes the local pointer `r`, not the global `head`. Should assign `head = temp` when inserting at front.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-3: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [This assignment `r = temp` only changes the local copy of r, not the global head or any node's next pointer, so the new node is not actually inserted into the list.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [This assignment only changes local variable r, not the global head or any node's next pointer, so the new node is not properly linked into the list when inserting at the beginning.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [This assignment only changes the local variable r, not the global head or any node's next pointer. Should assign to head when inserting at beginning.]
